and

Foundation fellowship and the ACM prize

Learners around the globe and today she

Science and Technology as founder of in

Daphne welcome
thank you it's a pleasure to be here
Sheamus and Daphne I gotta say that is
an incredible list of accomplishments
thank you so first question we have to
ask is tell us about your journey to
becoming one of the foremost experts in
the intersection of biology and ains
really what motivated you to pursue
expertise in both fields
so my Origins are actually in machine
learning and they date back to a time
when machine learning and AI in general
were
um let's just say marginalized in the in
the field of computer science I got my
PhD working in this space at a time but
you couldn't really say you were doing
AI you had to say you were doing
cognitive Computing because if you're
doing AI you're kind of like a little
bit out there which is kind of funny
when you think about where we are today
um I I was actually among the first
people to enter into the field of
machine learning from within AI I when I
started in the field many people were
still doing logical fear improving type
stuff and so I was the first machine
learning higher into Stanford's computer
science department all the way back in
1995 and I became interested in machine
learning in the service of biomedical
data because way back then the data sets
that were available in core machine
learning were actually not very exciting
and not very inspirational we're
thinking it was things like filtering
email messages into spam non-spam or
deciding a bunch of news group messages
which category they belonged in and they
were very small and not very
aspirational and this was around the
time in the late 90s that biology was
actually starting to measure itself in
larger scale ways so the first
microarray data sets were coming out the
first human genome was coming out in the
early 2000s and I felt like there were
incredible opportunities to do something
that was both more societally beneficial
and also more technologically
interesting and so in the first um few
years and this was um around the late
90s uh the first few years that I was in
that space I was mostly focused on it as
a technological challenge problem but
then I became interested in the field in
its own right and so throughout my uh
the majority of my Stanford career I
ended up actually having a bifurcated
existence where half my lab continued to
do core machine learning research
published in the standard computer
science venues icml neurops and so on
and the other half were published in
journals like Nature and Science and
cell and what was funny was that my
computer science colleagues most of them
didn't realize I did biology I still
have people walking up to me today
saying so what made you get into biology
after Coursera and it was like no it
actually happened like 20 some years ago
and I and the colleagues that I had in
biology didn't realize I could be in a
computer science department they assumed
I was in the medical school so it's very
interesting to see
um to have that sort of real bifurcation
of my research
yeah incredibly impressive and a great
inspiration for those people who want to
work in multiple Fields so
you're um I know you're doing lots of
things currently and but one of your
main focuses as a founder and head of um
which you mentioned introduction can you
describe the core mission and goals
obvious
so when you look at um the current state
of drug Discovery and unfortunately most
of us either ourselves or our loved ones
probably all of the above will end up
needing therapeutic intervention at some
point in our lives and you look at how
long and how complicated it is
um to make in medicine for a new for a
new indication
um and you and when you look at the
statistics for that you realize that
even of drug programs that actually
enter the clinic 90 of them fail what
um what other industry has a 90 failure
rate and so the reason I think is
because we are trying to intervene in a
system that we just don't understand uh
we don't Define diseases properly we
Define them typically using coarse
grained clinical symptomology very
subjective and then we try and do
interventions in these really complex
systems without truly understanding
what's going on
um I believe that the the challenges
that the human brain just cannot
understand the human biology and it's in
its complexity it's just a very
challenging problem and so we believe
that the path forward in order to make
meaningful therapeutic interventions is
to collect as much data as we can about
the Human Condition about the human
biology about cell biology and then put
that into a machine and let it do what
it currently does better than people can
which is understand subtle patterns
connect them to each other and then use
that to identify meaningful causal
interventions that could truly modulate
disease and they subset of humans and so
that's what we're trying to do trying to
collect a lot of data trying to build
machine learning methods capable of
interrogating it and using that to
translate into therapeutic interventions
you know fascinating so I do want to get
into your um comment about the um
failure rate of uh drug development
being so high but uh you know one one
quick comment to um a credit to you I
think um data Centric AI like now is all

of years ago
so when people didn't realize um or
we're starting to realize that the

the large language models and everything

and we started to talk about Ai and
biotech M can you explain the key

driven methodology which you just
um yeah referred to
so and let me just say that what I'll be
describing is a bit of a little bit of a
caricature because of course there's a
multitude of researchers working in drug
Discovery and there is a multitude of
approaches that they take so I'll be
providing kind of like a little bit of
an extreme version but typically drug
Discovery emerges from a biological
hypothesis that a scientist comes up
with by reading papers doing some
experiments and small model systems
saying what happens if I do this and you
basically follow a hypothesis driven
interrogation and ultimately the
scientists and and the um and the powers
of being a Pharma company develop enough
conviction around a particular
hypothesis and they take it through into
drug Discovery we take a very different
approach that is
anti-hypothesis driven we collect as
much data as we can about a particular
type of biological system that we know
is implicated in the disease process and
we go as unbiased as we possibly can so
for example when we collect data on
Cellular Systems we measure them with
all sorts of different measurements like
every possible Trend the level of every
possible Gene in the transcriptome like
what how how active is it we measure
cell morphology we measure cell
trajectories as they evolve over time as
unbiased as we possibly can and then we
use machine learning to get to have the
hypotheses emerge from the data and use
orthogonal forms of data to gain
conviction around those hypotheses so in
some sense rather than starting out with
a hypothesis and then doing experiments
to test those out we do experiments to
feed into machine learning models who
tell us what the interesting findings
the interesting insights that emerge
from those data and then we select among
those which are the ones that are most
likely to give rise to meaningful
therapeutic intervention
no extremely interesting and damn

um
prepping for this interview and reading
that the um

and so the main contributor to that
incredible cost is the fact that we that

um you put the molecule into a person

effects
um and though we've become pretty good

um and so that it's a relatively
few drugs failed phase one phase two is
where you first test out the biological
hypothesis that that made you go after
this Target in the first place and the
reason why most
um most drugs fail at phase two is
because that is the first time when we
realize that the Target that we've
selected to go after with the drug
actually doesn't modulate the clinical
outcome that we set out to modulate at
all so that's really where if you will
the rubber hits the road now phase three
is where you take the findings that you
bought from what is typically a lower
powered Phase 2 trial and really test
out that um that hypothesis in a much
larger population it's also the time
when some of those um sort of rare
events that cause toxicity come out
because you put the drug in a much
larger patient population gives you more
more of an opportunity for sort of
outlier people that interact in in
unexpectedly poor ways of the drug that
emerges but the main cause for failure
in phase three other than those
unexpected outlier events is people who
squint at the data in phase two and say
I didn't see the signal I was hoping for
but you know what I'm gonna try it
anyway
so
um so I think that's partly honestly a
matter of just having the rigor and um
and sort of willingness to say no uh to
pushing things forward that are less
likely to succeed
um but phase two is really where the
rubber hits the road so what we do you
know we're not in phase two as of yet or
even phase one but
um the failure in phase two begins a lot
earlier it begins when you first select
the Target and the patient population in
which you put your medicine
um so the fundamental biological
hypothesis that uh that in the even
incentivizes you to go and make a

Target and so a lot of our Discovery

in at this Target in a given population
of humans that that will modulate this

clinical hypothesis and
um and it's effectively making a

because when you make a you put a drug

so you can think of it as building a
sufficiently

that it can predict outcomes of causal

wow and

and sitro started in 2015 I believe

um so I'm sure you guys have addressed

so I think um if you go back to the

that we're looking to build predictive
models of causal interventions in humans
and we're doing it using machine
learning the first question you ask
yourself is we don't get to make causal

we do it's called a clinical trial and
by that point it's too late so how do
you get
um enough data around causal
interventions in humans in order to
build predictive models and in order to
do that we've taken a rather unusual
strategy of bringing together and
bridging between two very distinct and
complementary data modalities
um the first of those is data from
humans and as I said you don't have a
lot of ability to interrogate causality
in humans because
um human uh human trajectories are
invariably confounded
um
so uh with non-causal associations but
one very uh important ground truth that
we have around causality is human
genetics so when my genes are going One
Direction or another when there's a
particular variant in my genome
um it causes a certain
um phenotypic trait Downstream I mean
it's not perfect there's often a
combination of multiple genetic and
environmental factors but it is one of
those few cases where you are pretty
certain that the causality goes in One
Direction and not the other and so we
use data from Human genetics and complex
phenotypic traits that are measured in
humans
um and that's exactly to avoid the
hypothesis-driven uh sort of looking
under the Lamppost of we know what what
we believe we know what matters because
a clinician told us what matters we
deliberately put that aside and say
we're going to measure human biology in
unbiased ways ways that emerge from high
content modalities such as blood
biomarkers histopathology MRI imaging of
the brain or the body I mean lots of
high content data and then relating that
to the genetics as a kind of as a causal
root of this to understand the effect
between genotype and phenotype at the
human level the other form of data that
we put um together with that is data
from Cellular Systems that we generate
in our own in-house wet lab we take data
that are we take cells that are derived
from humans and we intervene in them
also genetically using Technologies such
as crispr in order to say if we make
this genetic intervention in a Cell what
cellular phenotype does it cause how
does that how does the cells change its
state when we make this genetic
intervention and the reason why we like
that is because
unlike in the human case here we
actually get to do an experiment on the
human data side it's the system that we
want to modify but we don't get the app
actually do causal experimentation on
the cellular system side we can do
causal experimentation it's in the wrong
system but at least it's related to the
right system so the machine learning is
used in our case in two ways both to
create a much a more meaningful
representation of these high content
data modalities in both humans and cells
and also to bridge between them so that
you have translatable models in the lab
that truly predict the effect of an
intervention in a human being
yeah that's a very good example of a
data-driven data Centric approach and so
one thing I would have to ask is like
how do air researchers in this field
like how do you generate these

um
you were talking about

actually long before October I mean it
was the actually the beginning of my

is when I looked around me at a point
when Coursera was kind of at a good

and even before large language models
there was the progression that we used

the reason is because we have lots and

generate massive data sets in biology
and so where do those come from on The

like the UK biobanks that are capturing

that study and so that
um was an incredible opportunity on the

there is a convergence of technologies
that cell biologists and bioengineers

few years to enable us to print the data
so you can think of of it is building an

just prints more and more data about
that cause effect relationship in cell
biology so those Technologies involve
things like as I mentioned crispr
um they involve the ability to create
human-derived Cellular Systems from what

we can take one of our skin cell and
revert it back to a stem cell status and
then create a neuron or a cardiomyocyte

can start but with our genetics so I can

neuronal development
I can measure that using super

um in a lot of cases Because by the time

um other things are more limited in

um and it's
